articleSummary: The article argues that for many read-heavy, rebuildable workloads (e.g., documentation, knowledge bases, prototype RAG systems), a new class of serverless, CDN-capable datastores—such as Pagefind, Orama, Chroma, LanceDB, and DuckDB-WASM—offers a simpler alternative to traditional databases. Its core thesis is that if data can be rebuilt from files and users mostly read it, storing indexes as files and serving them over HTTP is more practical than running a full database. The tone is analytical and decision-oriented, using the metaphor of "S3 cosplay" to warn against forcing object storage to act like a database for write-heavy workloads. The intended audience is developers and architects evaluating mid-scale (1,000–1,000,000 records) search and analytics solutions, with a focus on AI-driven semantic search and hybrid ranking.
